records.ety: [OE. betraien, bitraien; pref. be- + OF. trar to betray, F. trahir, fr. L. tradere. See Traitor.]
records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Betrayed (-trād"); p. pr. & vb. n. Betraying.]

defs: To deliver into the hands of an enemy by treachery or fraud, in violation of trust; to give up treacherously or faithlessly; as, an officer betrayed the city.
quotes: Jesus said unto them, The Son of man shall be betrayed into the hands of men.

defs: To prove faithless or treacherous to, as to a trust or one who trusts; to be false to; to deceive; as, to betray a person or a cause.
quotes: But when I rise, I shall find my legs betraying me.

defs: To violate the confidence of, by disclosing a secret, or that which one is bound in honor not to make known.
quotes: Willing to serve or betray any government for hire.

defs: To disclose or discover, as something which prudence would conceal; to reveal unintentionally.
quotes: Be swift to hear, but cautious of your tongue, lest you betray your ignorance.

defs: To mislead; to expose to inconvenience not foreseen to lead into error or sin.
quotes: Genius . . . often betrays itself into great errors.

defs: To lead astray, as a maiden; to seduce (as under promise of marriage) and then abandon.
To show or to indicate; -- said of what is not obvious at first, or would otherwise be concealed.
quotes: All the names in the country betray great antiquity.
